text: Invisibly returns data frame screening results, one row per variable columns including Base R type, jstats Class SubClass, classification Source ("registered" "structural"), distinct-value count, missing count percentage, outlier count (NA non-Numeric variables), Mean Median (NA meaningful: Median NA dichotomies, NA non-numeric-like variables). returned values raw counts; printed tables blank zeros omit clean rows.

text: jstats Class column reports package treats variable analyses (Numeric, Categorical, Numbers--text, Date-time, Unsupported), contrast Base R Type column's storage view; classification gates outlier screening, Numeric-class variables SD-screened Outliers cell left blank rest. Zero counts shown blank affected variables carry numbers; column (whole Missing/Outliers table) omitted entirely nothing flagged, header count lines explain omission. least one variable's class comes registration (jnumeric, jcount, jdummy) rather structural guess, Source column appears. reads exception-marker: "registered" shown registered variables structurally classified rows left blank, registrations stand glance. (returned data frame still records literal tier every row.) Set stats = TRUE ("mean" / "median") add central-tendency columns numeric-like variables: Numeric Count variables show Mean Median, numeric dichotomy shows raw mean stored codes blank median. numeric dichotomy coded 0/1 (e.g. 1/2 Group-4 coding) flagged "*" sub-class cell, since raw mean proportion; marker shows even stats , surfacing recode need. variable names supplied, variables screened. omitted, variables data frame screened. subset expression references variables already screening list, included automatically.

what: jstats: Simplified Statistical Analysis Tools for Social Science — jstats-package
title: jstats: Simplified Statistical Analysis Tools for Social Science — jstats-package
text: jstats simplifies R users need social science analyses without required become experienced computer programmers first. package provides consistent syntax, sensible defaults, protection confusing base R behaviors, staying close enough base R conventions users learn transferable skills rather private dialect. Output styled best conventions alternative applications SPSS, Stata, SAS, code syntax designed ease transition alternative packages R. package originally built teaching infrastructure university-level statistics course, now expanded broader social science research community.

title: jstats: Simplified Statistical Analysis Tools for Social Science — jstats-package
text: long-term primary audience broader social science quantitative research community – criminologists, sociologists, political scientists, psychologists, public health researchers, others routinely work Likert scales, categorical variables, dichotomies, Cronbach's alpha, dummy-coded regression, haven-imported data SPSS, Stata, SAS. current development phase package tested actively students colleagues Griffith University, plus growing community former students collaborating instructors. Feedback group shapes ongoing refinements.

title: jstats: Simplified Statistical Analysis Tools for Social Science — jstats-package
text: Descriptive analysis jdesc – univariate descriptives (mean, median, SD, range, etc.) optional grouping jfreq – frequency tables one variables jcorr – Pearson Spearman correlations significance tests jalpha – Cronbach's alpha item-total statistics scale reliability jscreen – data screening outliers, ranges, skew Group comparisons modeling jt – independent paired t-test jaov – one-way analysis variance optional post-hoc tests jcrosstab – cross-tabulation chi-square effect-size options jlm – linear regression jlogistic – logistic regression Variable construction jrecode – recode values, optional new value labels jrelabel – apply replace value labels variable label jsum – row-wise sum across variables, min-valid handling javg – row-wise mean across variables, min-valid handling Pipeline state management juse – set default data frame used implicitly analysis functions jsubset – activate row-level case-selection expression applied subsequent calls jcomplete – activate listwise filtering selected variables jdummy – register categorical variables dummy coding regression joutput – set session-level output verbosity (minimal / standard / full) Data import export jload – load data .rds, .sav, .dta, .sas7bdat, .xlsx, .csv jsave – save data frame, format inferred file extension Visualisation jplot – base histograms bar plots data, plus method dispatch result objects jt(), jlm(), etc. full alphabetical listing every exported function, run library(help = "jstats") browse package index.

title: jstats: Simplified Statistical Analysis Tools for Social Science — jstats-package
text: j-prefix. Every user-facing function starts j, package's whole API can discovered RStudio typing j pressing Tab. Internal helpers begin dot .jst_ intended direct use. Formula vs data-first. Group-comparison modeling functions follow base R formula interface: jt(MathScore ~ Gender, data = SampleData). Descriptive data-management functions take data frame first, followed unquoted variable names: jfreq(SampleData, Gender, Program). matches conventions base R functions like aggregate() cor(). juse-first habit. single juse(MyData) call start session sets default data frame. Subsequent analysis calls can omit data argument: jfreq(Gender) works jfreq(MyData, Gender). default also scopes pipeline-state functions, jsubset(Age < 30) sets filter current default without specification. Pipeline stages. jsubset(), jcomplete(), jdummy() modify session state subsequent analysis calls read automatically. State explicit – calls can inspected, inactivated, cleared, active state reported analysis output, script's behavior stays visible reproducible rather depending hidden context. Output verbosity. joutput() sets one three preset levels – minimal, standard (default), full – modulate much detail analysis functions print. Useful stripping output production scripts expanding exploration. Per-call arguments always override session-level settings. Case Processing Summary table follows auto-suppress rule standard tier: prints something happened (pipeline state, listwise drops, per-variable discrepancy notification) stays silent otherwise. See ?joutput full toggle behavior.

what: Set, activate, deactivate, or clear a per-dataset case-selection expression — jsubset
title: Set, activate, deactivate, or clear a per-dataset case-selection expression — jsubset
text: jsubset() sets persistent case-selection expression applied automatically jstats analysis functions default data frame (set juse()) use. analogous SPSS FILTER command. expression stored per dataset, switching juse() datasets preserves dataset's setting independently. expression applies whenever matching dataset used, regardless whether supplied via juse() specified explicitly function call. bypass temporarily without losing , use jsubset() analysis jsubset() afterward. matches SPSS FILTER / USE convention. Expressions use standard R logical operators: ==, !=, <, <=, >, >=, & (), | (), ! (), xor() (XOR), %%. Using = equality SPSS-style keywords //produce helpful error suggesting correct R syntax.

title: Set, activate, deactivate, or clear a per-dataset case-selection expression — jsubset
Set, activate, deactivate, or clear a per-dataset case-selection expression — jsubset
text: data Optional data frame. supplied, expression stored dataset specifically. omitted, dataset set juse() used. expr logical expression (e.g. Age < 40 & Gender == 1), one following special values: Deactivate setting remember expression. Reactivate previously deactivated setting. NULL Clear setting entirely (forget expression). expr data omitted, prints current jsubset status.

title: Set, activate, deactivate, or clear a per-dataset case-selection expression — jsubset
text: Invisibly returns NULL. Called side effect.

title: Set, activate, deactivate, or clear a per-dataset case-selection expression — jsubset
code: juse(community) #> Default data frame set to: community jsubset(Age < 40)                        # Set using juse default #> jsubset activated for community: Age < 40 jsubset(community, Age < 40)             # Explicit dataset #> jsubset activated for community: Age < 40 jsubset(Age < 40 & WellbeingScore > 50)  # Compound condition #> jsubset replaced for community: Age < 40 & WellbeingScore > 50 (was: Age < 40) jsubset(off)                             # Deactivate #> jsubset deactivated for community. jsubset(on)                              # Reactivate #> jsubset reactivated for community: Age < 40 & WellbeingScore > 50 jsubset()                                # Check status #> jsubset active for community: Age < 40 & WellbeingScore > 50 jsubset(NULL)                            # Clear entirely #> jsubset cleared for community (had: Age < 40 & WellbeingScore > 50). # Not normally needed. You'd clear a default or registration only to # undo a mistake, or -- as in this example -- to reset state for testing. juse(NULL) #> Default data frame cleared.

what: Compute a row-wise sum across multiple variables — jsum
title: Compute a row-wise sum across multiple variables — jsum
text: jsum() computes sum values across multiple variables case (row) data frame. typically used create composite scores set related items (e.g. summing 6 survey items total scale score). default, cases missing values receive NA. Use min.valid argument allow partial sums — example, min.valid = 1 returns sum available values long least one item non-missing. Variables can listed individually using colon notation select range consecutive columns (e.g. Attitude1:Attitude6).

title: Compute a row-wise sum across multiple variables — jsum
Compute a row-wise sum across multiple variables — jsum
text: data data frame, omit use juse() default. ... Unquoted variable names. Use colon notation (e.g. Attitude1:Attitude6) select range consecutive columns. min.valid Integer (optional). minimum number non-missing values required compute sum. case fewer non-missing values, result NA. omitted, values must non-missing (equivalent setting min.valid number variables). var.label Character string (optional). variable label attach result. omitted, auto-generated label used.

title: Compute a row-wise sum across multiple variables — jsum
text: numeric vector length nrow(data), suitable assigning new column: MyData$Total <- jsum(Var1, Var2, Var3).

title: Compute a row-wise sum across multiple variables — jsum
code: # Set the default data frame (so you can omit it in function calls) juse(community) #> Default data frame set to: community  # Sum three variables (all must be non-missing) community$EnvTotal <- jsum(Environment1, Environment3, Environment4) #> Sum of 3 variables computed for 100 cases (18 set to NA due to missing values). #> Mean of the new variable: 9.220. #>  #> Note: jsum() returns the totals; assign them to a column to keep them: #>   community$<name> <- jsum(...) #> For the full distribution (min, max, SD), run jdesc() on the new column.  # Sum with partial data allowed (at least 2 non-missing) community$EnvTotal <- jsum(Environment1, Environment3, Environment4,                            min.valid = 2) #> Sum of 3 variables computed for 100 cases (min.valid = 2: 12 cases used partial data, 6 set to NA due to missing values). #> Mean of the new variable: 8.904. #>  #> Note: jsum() returns the totals; assign them to a column to keep them: #>   community$<name> <- jsum(...) #> For the full distribution (min, max, SD), run jdesc() on the new column.  # Sum using colon range for consecutive columns community$EnvTotal <- jsum(Environment1:Environment5) #> Sum of 5 variables computed for 100 cases (18 set to NA due to missing values). #> Mean of the new variable: 14.976. #>  #> Note: jsum() returns the totals; assign them to a column to keep them: #>   community$<name> <- jsum(...) #> For the full distribution (min, max, SD), run jdesc() on the new column.  # Mix colon ranges and explicit names (e.g. after reverse-coding an item) community$Environment2R <- jrecode(community, Environment2,                                    map = "1=5; 2=4; 3=3; 4=2; 5=1") #>  #> Note: jrecode() returns the recoded values; assign them to a column to keep them: #>   community$<name> <- jrecode(...) #> To check the recode landed correctly, compare jfreq() on the original and the new column. community$ScaleTotal <- jsum(Environment1, Environment2R,                              Environment3:Environment5) #> Sum of 5 variables computed for 100 cases (18 set to NA due to missing values). #> Mean of the new variable: 15.415. #>  #> Note: jsum() returns the totals; assign them to a column to keep them: #>   community$<name> <- jsum(...) #> For the full distribution (min, max, SD), run jdesc() on the new column.  # With a custom variable label community$ScaleTotal <- jsum(Environment1:Environment5,                              var.label = "Environment Scale Total") #> Sum of 5 variables computed for 100 cases (18 set to NA due to missing values). #> Mean of the new variable: 14.976. #>  #> Note: jsum() returns the totals; assign them to a column to keep them: #>   community$<name> <- jsum(...) #> For the full distribution (min, max, SD), run jdesc() on the new column.  # With an explicit data frame (instead of using juse default) community$EnvTotal <- jsum(community, Environment1, Environment3,                            Environment4) #> Sum of 3 variables computed for 100 cases (18 set to NA due to missing values). #> Mean of the new variable: 9.220. #>  #> Note: jsum() returns the totals; assign them to a column to keep them: #>   community$<name> <- jsum(...) #> For the full distribution (min, max, SD), run jdesc() on the new column.  # Not normally needed. You'd clear a default or registration only to # undo a mistake, or -- as in this example -- to reset state for testing. juse(NULL) #> Default data frame cleared.

what: Independent samples or paired samples t-test — jt
title: Independent samples or paired samples t-test — jt
text: Runs t-test prints formatted group descriptives test results. default, runs traditional Student's independent samples t-test assuming equal variances. Optional parameters provide Welch's correction, paired samples, effect size (Cohen's d), Levene's test, confidence interval mean difference. Handles haven-labelled, numeric, factor grouping variables. haven-labelled variables, numeric codes displayed alongside labels group descriptives table.

title: Independent samples or paired samples t-test — jt
code: jt(   formula,   data,   paired = FALSE,   welch = FALSE,   effect.size = NULL,   levene = NULL,   ci = NULL,   subset = NULL,   variable.id = NULL,   value.id = NULL,   case.processing.detail = NULL,   full = FALSE,   digits = NULL )

title: Independent samples or paired samples t-test — jt
text: formula formula form DV ~ Group. data data frame containing variables referenced formula. paired Logical. TRUE, runs paired samples t-test. two groups must equal sample sizes. Default FALSE. welch Logical. FALSE (default), runs Student's t-test (equal variances assumed). TRUE, runs Welch's t-test. Ignored paired = TRUE. effect.size Logical NULL. TRUE, prints Cohen's d. NULL (default), defers joutput() session setting. levene Logical NULL. TRUE, prints Levene's test homogeneity variance. Ignored paired = TRUE. NULL (default), defers joutput(). ci Logical NULL. TRUE, adds 95% confidence interval mean difference. NULL (default), defers joutput(). subset optional unquoted logical expression (e.g. Group == 1) subset cases call . Applied jcomplete jsubset. affect function calls. variable.id Character NULL. Variable label display mode: one "", "names", "labels", "legend", "legend.bottom". "names" shows variable names ; "" shows "name: label"; "labels" shows DV grouping-variable labels table captions (group levels follow value.id mode) – best short labels; "legend"/"legend.bottom" keep names print label legend output. NULL (default) defers joutput()'s variable.id setting. logical. value.id Character NULL. Value-label display mode group descriptives rows: "" ("code: label"), "values" (bare code), "labels" (label, degrading bare code code none). "legend" "legend.bottom" keep bare code table print value-label legend ("legend" per-table, "legend.bottom" consolidated multiple tables produced). -op grouping variables value labels. NULL (default) defers joutput()'s value.id setting. logical. case.processing.detail Per-call override Case Processing Summary detail tier: one "none", "totals", "per_code". NULL (default) uses active joutput() level default. full Logical. TRUE, turns effect.size, levene, ci . override explicit FALSE values. digits Integer NULL. Number decimal places continuous statistics output tables (range 0-7; digits = 0 prints whole numbers trailing decimal point). affect p-values, percentages, integer quantities (counts, N, degrees freedom), keep fixed conventions. NULL (default) defers joutput()'s digits setting (default 3).

title: Independent samples or paired samples t-test — jt
text: Invisibly returns list class jst_ttest containing: model (t.test result), model_frame (analysis data frame used plotting), test_type, formula, descriptives, t, df, p, mean_difference, ci (95% CI), cohens_d, d_label, n, sample_info (pipeline missing data counts).

title: Independent samples or paired samples t-test — jt
text: red title identifying test type printed first, followed variable labels (present), results tables.

title: Independent samples or paired samples t-test — jt
code: # With explicit data frame jt(WellbeingScore ~ Volunteer, data = community) #> Independent Samples T-Test #> Group Descriptives: WellbeingScore by Volunteer #> Group    N    Mean      SD #> ------  --  ------  ------ #> 0: No   58  46.431  10.318 #> 1: Yes  42  56.357  10.385 #>  #> Independent Samples T-Test Results (equal variances assumed) #>      t  df  p      Mean Difference  95% CI Lower  95% CI Upper #> ------  --  -----  ---------------  ------------  ------------ #> -4.735  98  <.001           -9.926       -14.086        -5.766 #>  #> Cohen's d: -0.959 #>  jt(WellbeingScore ~ Volunteer, data = community, welch = TRUE) #> Welch's Independent Samples T-Test #> Group Descriptives: WellbeingScore by Volunteer #> Group    N    Mean      SD #> ------  --  ------  ------ #> 0: No   58  46.431  10.318 #> 1: Yes  42  56.357  10.385 #>  #> Welch's T-Test Results (equal variances not assumed) #>     t    df  p      Mean Difference  95% CI Lower  95% CI Upper #> -----  ----  -----  ---------------  ------------  ------------ #> -4.73  88.2  <.001           -9.926       -14.096        -5.756 #>  #> Cohen's d: -0.959 #>  jt(WellbeingScore ~ Volunteer, data = community, full = TRUE) #> Independent Samples T-Test #> Levene's Test for Homogeneity of Variance #> F  df1  df2  p    #> -  ---  ---  ---- #> 0    1   98  .996 #>  #> Group Descriptives: WellbeingScore by Volunteer #> Group    N    Mean      SD #> ------  --  ------  ------ #> 0: No   58  46.431  10.318 #> 1: Yes  42  56.357  10.385 #>  #> Independent Samples T-Test Results (equal variances assumed) #>      t  df  p      Mean Difference  95% CI Lower  95% CI Upper #> ------  --  -----  ---------------  ------------  ------------ #> -4.735  98  <.001           -9.926       -14.086        -5.766 #>  #> Cohen's d: -0.959 #>   # Using juse() default juse(community) #> Default data frame set to: community jt(WellbeingScore ~ Volunteer) #> Independent Samples T-Test #> Using default data frame: community #> Group Descriptives: WellbeingScore by Volunteer #> Group    N    Mean      SD #> ------  --  ------  ------ #> 0: No   58  46.431  10.318 #> 1: Yes  42  56.357  10.385 #>  #> Independent Samples T-Test Results (equal variances assumed) #>      t  df  p      Mean Difference  95% CI Lower  95% CI Upper #> ------  --  -----  ---------------  ------------  ------------ #> -4.735  98  <.001           -9.926       -14.086        -5.766 #>  #> Cohen's d: -0.959 #>  jt(WellbeingScore ~ Volunteer, full = TRUE) #> Independent Samples T-Test #> Using default data frame: community #> Levene's Test for Homogeneity of Variance #> F  df1  df2  p    #> -  ---  ---  ---- #> 0    1   98  .996 #>  #> Group Descriptives: WellbeingScore by Volunteer #> Group    N    Mean      SD #> ------  --  ------  ------ #> 0: No   58  46.431  10.318 #> 1: Yes  42  56.357  10.385 #>  #> Independent Samples T-Test Results (equal variances assumed) #>      t  df  p      Mean Difference  95% CI Lower  95% CI Upper #> ------  --  -----  ---------------  ------------  ------------ #> -4.735  98  <.001           -9.926       -14.086        -5.766 #>  #> Cohen's d: -0.959 #>

what: Set or display the default data frame for jstats functions — juse
title: Set or display the default data frame for jstats functions — juse
text: juse() sets default data frame used automatically jstats functions data argument omitted. reduces typing makes interactive use convenient. function stores name data frame, copy data. means changes make data frame (adding columns, recoding variables, etc.) automatically reflected subsequent function calls.

title: Set or display the default data frame for jstats functions — juse
Set or display the default data frame for jstats functions — juse
text: data data frame (unquoted). omitted, prints current default. Use juse(NULL) clear default.

title: Set or display the default data frame for jstats functions — juse
text: Invisibly returns NULL. Called side effect setting, displaying, clearing default data frame.

title: Set or display the default data frame for jstats functions — juse
text: juse() stores name data frame, copy data. means changes make data frame (adding columns, recoding variables, etc.) automatically reflected subsequent function calls. differs base R's attach(), creates snapshot can become stale modifications. juse() recommended approach package.

title: Set or display the default data frame for jstats functions — juse
code: juse(community)              # Set community as the default #> Default data frame set to: community juse()                       # Display current default #> Current default data frame: community jdesc(Age, WellbeingScore)   # Uses community automatically #> Descriptive Statistics #> Using default data frame: community #>  #> Variable        Total  Non_missing  Min  Max   Mean      SD #> --------------  -----  -----------  ---  ---  -----  ------ #> Age               100          100   18   71  40.66  11.680 #> WellbeingScore    100          100   27   77  50.60  11.411 #>  juse(NULL)                   # Clear the default #> Default data frame cleared.
